related_chapters.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
related_chapters.chapter_title: It Was Not Spontaneous
related_chapters.why: Cooper argues that the education and organized agency of Black women drive racial progress, and Terry credits the women of Bennett College as the deliberate planners of the Greensboro sit-in.

related_chapters.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton
related_chapters.part_title: The Only Woman at the Table
related_chapters.chapter_title: The Only Woman at the Table
related_chapters.why: Cooper insists a woman's voice belongs in the councils that shape her people's future, and Cotton recounts holding her place as the only woman in SCLC's inner leadership circle.

related_chapters.subject: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson
related_chapters.part_title: Strong Women, and the Deacon Ranks
related_chapters.chapter_title: A Movement Where Women Had a Voice
related_chapters.why: Cooper's central claim is that the race rises only as its women are heard, and this chapter turns directly on the place women held in the Bogalusa movement.

related_chapters.subject: Grace Hall Miller
related_chapters.part_title: Shirley Sherrod and a Family's History
related_chapters.chapter_title: If You Don't Know Your History
related_chapters.topic: Teaching the Young and a Legacy
related_chapters.why: Cooper ties women's leadership to teaching the next generation, and Miller, mother of Shirley Sherrod, speaks of passing history and purpose to the young.

related_chapters.subject: Judy Richardson
related_chapters.chapter_title: No Minutes Until Freedom Comes
related_chapters.why: Cooper frames women as agents rather than bystanders of reform, and this chapter centers on a sit-in carried by women in the SNCC office.

related_chapters.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons
related_chapters.part_title: Waiting on You All My Life
related_chapters.chapter_title: She Was the Driving Force
related_chapters.topic: Two Women Who Took Them In
related_chapters.why: Cooper credits unheralded women as the engine of community uplift, and Simmons, a Freedom Summer project director, names the women who sustained the Laurel project.

top_voice.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
top_voice.reason: Terry joins Cooper's argument that educated Black women are the deliberate authors of progress: a Bennett College student in the Greensboro sit-in who became a chair of Afro-American Studies.

related_chapters.subject: Ellie Dahmer
related_chapters.chapter_title: We Stopped Sleeping in Shifts
related_chapters.why: Wells documents racial terror against families who claimed their rights, and Dahmer recounts the night watches before the Klan firebombing that killed her husband, the Forrest County NAACP president.

related_chapters.subject: Clarence Magee
related_chapters.part_title: Legacy and the Work That Remains
related_chapters.chapter_title: A Great-Uncle Lynched, and Restorative Justice
related_chapters.why: Wells compiled the record of lynching to force a reckoning, and Magee speaks of a great-uncle lynched and of pursuing justice for it.

related_chapters.subject: Cecil J. Williams
related_chapters.chapter_title: Bearing Witness for Future Historians
related_chapters.why: Wells's method was documentary witness against racial violence, and Williams describes photographing that violence so a future record could not be denied.

related_chapters.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
related_chapters.chapter_title: The Ten-Gauge Shotgun as Self-Defense
related_chapters.why: Wells closed The Red Record arguing a Winchester in the home was a Black family's rightful protection, and Avery speaks plainly of the shotgun as self-defense.

related_chapters.subject: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson
related_chapters.chapter_title: Protecting the Community
related_chapters.why: Wells held that an unprotected people must defend itself, and the Andersons describe the Deacons for Defense organizing armed protection in Bogalusa.

related_chapters.subject: Euvester Simpson
related_chapters.chapter_title: Walk With Me, Lord
related_chapters.topic: A Washcloth and a Gospel Song
related_chapters.why: Wells records the suffering inflicted in custody on Black people charged with nothing, and Simpson recounts the night of the Winona jail beating she endured with Fannie Lou Hamer.

top_voice.subject: Ellie Dahmer
top_voice.reason: Dahmer's testimony is the living counterpart to Wells's ledger of racial murder: she survived the Klan firebombing that killed her husband for offering to pay others' poll taxes.

related_chapters.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
related_chapters.part_title: Tuskegee and a Black Officer's War
related_chapters.chapter_title: Up at Four to Milk the Cows
related_chapters.why: Washington built Tuskegee on students working dairies and trades to fund and learn, and Bassett describes that exact work-study labor as a Tuskegee student.

related_chapters.subject: Clarence Magee
related_chapters.chapter_title: A Father Who Vowed His Children Would Read
related_chapters.why: Washington grounds racial advance in education paired with self-sufficiency, and Magee recounts a sharecropper father who tied literacy to the family's independence.

related_chapters.subject: Charles McLaurin
related_chapters.chapter_title: A Grandmother's Books and Booker T. Washington
related_chapters.why: This chapter names Booker T. Washington directly as McLaurin recounts a grandmother's books shaping him, the schooling-and-self-improvement creed the essay advances.

related_chapters.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
related_chapters.chapter_title: Cooperatives and Liberty House
related_chapters.why: Washington argues an economic foundation precedes other gains, and Derby describes building craft cooperatives for Black economic development in Mississippi.

related_chapters.subject: Eddie Holloway
related_chapters.chapter_title: Get Your Lesson
related_chapters.why: Washington holds that learning is the lever that lifts a people, and Holloway frames education itself as the route to freedom.

related_chapters.subject: Harold K. Brown
related_chapters.chapter_title: How Much Did Your Shoes Cost
related_chapters.why: Washington urges thrift and capital-building over display, and Brown's reflection on a generation's misplaced spending priorities runs along the same line.

top_voice.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
top_voice.reason: Bassett embodies Washington's Tuskegee model carried to its furthest end: a Tuskegee work-study student who rose through hands-on science to a doctorate in dairy technology.

related_chapters.subject: C. T. Vivian
related_chapters.chapter_title: A Pastor's Savings and a Seminary
related_chapters.why: Du Bois traces Crummell's path into the priesthood through hardship, and Vivian recounts his own answer to a call to the ministry and entry into seminary.

related_chapters.subject: Amos C. Brown
related_chapters.chapter_title: J. Pius Barber and the Prophetic Tradition
related_chapters.why: Du Bois casts Crummell as a seer in the prophetic line, and Brown speaks of the prophetic tradition passed to him by an elder minister.

related_chapters.subject: John Churchville
related_chapters.chapter_title: The Gospel to Empower or to Keep Down
related_chapters.topic: A Thesis on Religion and Liberation
related_chapters.why: Crummell wrestled with whether faith would liberate or pacify his people, and Churchville's thesis weighs the gospel as either empowerment or suppression.

related_chapters.subject: Joseph Echols Lowery
related_chapters.chapter_title: We Were Outmanned
related_chapters.why: Du Bois renders Crummell as a churchman whose faith answered worldly defeat, and Lowery, an SCLC co-founder, speaks of the sacred roots that sustained an outmatched movement.

related_chapters.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
related_chapters.part_title: The Seminary and the Call to Selma
related_chapters.chapter_title: Seminary Discussions and the Protector Impulse
related_chapters.why: Crummell's life turns on a seminarian wrestling with vocation against the world's resistance, and Ackerman recounts the seminary debates over heeding the call to Selma.

related_chapters.subject: Carrie Lamar Young
related_chapters.chapter_title: Church, Singing, and Salvation
related_chapters.why: Du Bois sets Crummell's strength in the church that formed him, and Young grounds her own endurance in church, singing, and faith.

top_voice.subject: C. T. Vivian
top_voice.reason: Vivian is the closest living echo of Du Bois's Crummell: a minister who answered a religious calling and carried a prophetic vocation across a lifetime of struggle.

related_chapters.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
related_chapters.chapter_title: Refusing Lifelong Nonviolence at Shaw
related_chapters.why: Du Bois models honest dissent from a dominant leader's program, and McDew recounts publicly refusing to accept nonviolence as a creed at SNCC's founding.

related_chapters.subject: Charles McLaurin
related_chapters.chapter_title: A Grandmother's Books and Booker T. Washington
related_chapters.why: This essay is Du Bois's direct reckoning with Booker T. Washington, whom McLaurin names as a formative influence through a grandmother's books.

related_chapters.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
related_chapters.chapter_title: Sterling's Old Gang
related_chapters.why: Du Bois insists on the higher education Washington deprioritized, and Terry recounts building a university Afro-American Studies department under a scholarly mentor.

related_chapters.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
related_chapters.chapter_title: The University of the Movement
related_chapters.why: Du Bois prizes the training of leaders and thinkers, and Sellers, a college president, reflects on the movement itself as a university that formed a generation.

related_chapters.subject: Junius Williams
related_chapters.part_title: A Mayoral Race and a Return to Organizing
related_chapters.chapter_title: Your Voice Deserves to Be Heard
related_chapters.why: Du Bois holds that a people must cultivate its own voice and leaders, and Williams describes teaching a new generation that its voice deserves to be heard.

related_chapters.subject: Harold K. Brown
related_chapters.chapter_title: How Much Did Your Shoes Cost
related_chapters.why: Du Bois engages Washington's economic gospel critically, and Brown weighs an economic program against a generation's values in the same register of debate.

top_voice.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
top_voice.reason: McDew enacts the essay's core move, principled disagreement with a leading figure's prescribed program, by refusing to make nonviolence a lifelong creed at SNCC's founding.
